data.title: Ginebra San Miguel: Strong 2024 Financial Performance And An Unintended Government-Provided Moat
data.description:
Introduction

Ginebra San Miguel, Inc. (GBSMF), one of the leading alcohol beverage companies in the Philippines and the biggest in the gin category, just released its 2024 full-year earnings on March 17, 2025, Philippine Time.

For the year, the company continues to demonstrate robust operating and financial performance, which I will discuss in further detail below, and based on my further analysis of the competitive landscape, it will likely continue to perform for years to come. This article will serve as a follow-up to my first one, where I laid out my investment thesis. Aside from Ginebra's 2024 financial performance, I delve into the Philippine Sin Tax Law that was signed into law by then-President Rodrigo Duterte. While the bill looks to be a headwind to the industry as it discourages consumers from consuming more alcohol, I believe that this regulation actually benefits Ginebra due to its economy of scale that can deter smaller entrants due to the high fixed cost associated with the industry.

With a 2024 operating profit of ₱8.6B and an enterprise value of ₱71.33B, this translates to around 12% earnings yield or an 8.3x EV/EBIT, which is a good deal considering you are buying a market leader in the Philippine spirit market with favorable economics due to regulation. Thus, I will maintain a 'Buy' rating for this stock.

2024 Full-Year Earnings Takeaway

For the full year, Ginebra generated revenue of ₱62.50B, an operating profit of around ₱8.6B, and a net income of ₱7.25B, an increase of 17%, 26%, and 3%, respectively, when compared to the prior year.

Ginebra's Operating margin expanded from 12.73% in 2023 to 13.75% in 2024, while its net margin declined from 13.14% to 11.61% in the same period. The decline in net margin can be attributed to the one-time gain from the sale of Don Papa. Without the one-time gain from Don Papa, the net margin would have expanded from 10.98% to 11.61% and would have increased by 23.21% from ₱5.89B to ₱7.26B.

For its future growth, Ginebra's management plans to expand its distribution network further in the Visayas and Mindanao Areas and intensify its marketing campaigns in those areas. This is a prudent choice as their retail partners are still underpenetrated in those areas and could have a growth potential once economic conditions in those areas improve. Furthermore, the price range that Ginebra competes in is perfect for those areas as these underdeveloped places likely won't have the financial capability for premium or upscale spirit brands.

To summarize, I believe this is a magnificent performance from Ginebra. Its operating margin continues to expand as the sales volume continues to increase. With the Philippine GDP growth as a tailwind on its top line, I believe that there will be further growth for Ginebra in the foreseeable future without going international, where it faces intense competition. Additionally, Ginebra's strong profitability, brand equity, and cost leadership due to its economy of scale will continue to be a competitive advantage in the Philippines. Additionally, there is the regulation, which from the outset looks to be a headwind for the Philippine spirit market. However, I believe this regulation actually makes it harder for entrants to challenge Ginebra's dominant position.

How Sin Tax Law Puts Ginebra In A Favorable Competitive Position

On January 22, 2020, Philippine then-President Rodrigo Duterte signed the Sin Tax bill into law. The law's objective is to raise money for the implementation of the Universal Health Act and encourage reduced consumption of cigarettes and Alcohol in the Philippines. The effect of the Sin Tax Law is clearly reflected in Ginebra's gross margin, which has declined since 2020.

This decline is attributed to the annual increase in the excise tax on alcoholic drinks, which accounts for a significant portion of Ginebra's cost of sales.

Despite this disturbing trend on their gross margin, I believe that Ginebra has chosen not to fully pass on the excise tax increase to consumers as they have other measures, especially on their operating side, that can offset their declining gross margin without a significant price increase.

Instead, by maintaining a price that wouldn't abuse the price inelasticity of its products, Ginebra was able to increase its sales volume. This allowed it to spread the cost per unit among its fixed operating expenses, such as maintaining the logistics fleet and marketing campaigns. This resulted in an operating margin expansion for the company.

This is also evident in the chart below, which shows that revenue growth has clearly outpaced the increase in overhead costs and selling and marketing costs.

With the annual increase in excise tax per proof liter set to ease at around 6% per year onwards, I believe there could be room for gross margin expansion for Ginebra moving forward. It could gradually increase its prices as consumer purchasing power continues to increase and inflation normalizes without significantly disrupting the buying tendency of its customers.

Despite the continued effort of the Philippine government to reduce alcohol spirit consumption, which from the outset is a headwind for the industry, these regulatory hurdles actually benefit Ginebra due to its economy of scale and entrenched leadership position in the spirit market. The annual increase in excise tax on alcoholic beverages will make it harder for local and international entrants that lack the scale in the Philippines to be price-competitive, which protects Ginebra's low-cost position. Aside from trying to reach a certain sales volume to achieve the economy of scale needed in manufacturing, which can be a challenge due to Ginebra's market presence and its significant resources and profitability to outinvest its competitors, entrants also need to cover for the fixed operating cost associated with consistent marketing to become and remain relevant and distribution. Just like in manufacturing, logistics must reach a certain scale before it becomes economically feasible to maintain. This further increases the fixed cost that entrants need to cover in order to compete effectively against Ginebra.

I believe that the mass-market price segment of the Philippines spirit market will continue to be dominated by incumbents with dominant market share in their respective spirit categories: Ginebra San Miguel in Gin, Emperador and Alfonso in brandy, and Tanduay in rum. The segment I could see entrants targeting is the premium or upscale one, as they could offset the high fixed cost through premium prices. Ginebra is also positioning itself in this emerging segment as there is a clear trend of Filipinos turning to premium spirit drinks due to the continued increase in the quality of living in the Philippines. While there won't be synergy in marketing and distribution due to the channel differences—one is mass-market, and the other is upscale—Ginebra has the resources and profitability to build its premium offering and, more importantly, the patience since time is very important in nurturing brand equity among consumers, especially the upscale ones that seek exclusivity and uniqueness.

Valuation

With an operating profit of around ₱8.6B and an enterprise value of around ₱71.33B, this translates to around 12% earnings yield or around 8.3x EV/EBIT multiple. Ginebra's balance sheet currently shows a significant cash pile-up, which explains the discrepancy with its current P/E ratio of around 11.75x. Ginebra currently has a cash surplus of around ₱14B if you subtract its total debt from its cash & cash equivalents. However, dividends are also increasing year by year due to the significant amount of cash the operations are generating lately versus their reinvestment rate or the generated cash they are reinvesting back into the business.

Since 2019, Ginebra has been reinvesting an average of around 12% of its operating profit back into the business. If the company keeps generating this amount of cash without reinvestment growth opportunities, there will likely be a big payout in the future. Thus, I believe that this cash in the balance sheet will eventually be paid out to the shareholders through increased dividends or share buybacks. Therefore, I will maintain a 'Buy' rating for Ginebra.

Where To Buy Ginebra San Miguel

Whenever my article is about a Philippine stock, I will put a special section here for investors looking to invest in the Philippine stock market. To invest in the Philippines, you have two options: Invest in Ginebra's OTC through your broker with a stock ticker symbol of 'GBSMF' or directly in the Philippine stock exchange or PSE by depositing your money with a trusted Philippine stockbroker such as COL Financial with a stock ticker of 'GSMI.' I will put this link here; COL Financial provided a guide here for foreign investors. Now, if you can stomach the volatility due to low volume and liquidity and will invest for the long term without looking at the stock price, then go for the ADR. Suppose you like looking at your portfolio every day and can't stand an overnight double-digit decline. In that case, you can invest directly by depositing your money directly with a Philippine broker. The liquidity and volume in the Philippine stock market, especially the highly popular stocks such as Ginebra, is high; the bid-and-ask spread is very close. If you worry about trust, you can go for popular and established stockbrokers such as COL Financial, which has been in operation since 1999. They are one of the most trusted brokers in the Philippines. Also, these stockbrokers are heavily regulated by the Philippine SEC and PSE.

Risk

Majority-Owned Company

Like many other public companies in the Philippines, Ginebra is majority-owned by its parent company or a powerful family, San Miguel Food and Beverage (SMPFF), which is owned by the ultimate parent, San Miguel Corporation (SMGBF). Due to the significant control of the company, they could undergo initiatives that could harm the well-being of the minority holder. A good example is companies piling cash on their balance sheet instead of distributing it back to the shareholders through buybacks or dividends even though there is a clear lack of growth opportunity for that idle cash, which can generate a superior above-cost capital return, which can also negatively affect the company's return on equity. In the US equity market, this is not that common anymore since you have corrective forces such as activist investors, private equity, and hedge funds on the lookout for these arbitrage opportunities, ready to buy a stake in the company and force the board to distribute their cash pile-ups through dividends or share buybacks. In the Philippine equity market, these corrective forces aren't available due to the lack of maturity of the equity market. Another good example is companies that don't do anything to unlock shareholder value, which can lead to their stock price declining to a deep discount level, even though the underlying business fundamentals remain strong. The company will instead choose to go private and buy back the shares of its minority shareholders at a deep discount. This is why I only invest in businesses that have a dividend policy and a yield that is competitive with the risk-free rate in the Philippines. The dividend yield will usually support the price from a further downside as long as the company can support that payout.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GigaCloud: No Bottom In Sight - More Uncertainties Ahead
data.description:
GCT's Prospects Remains Mixed, Attributed To The Uncertain Macro/Geopolitical Environment

We previously covered GigaCloud Technology Inc. (GCT) in December 2024, discussing its robust performance metrics and growing profitability, as its new Branding As A Service [BAAS] took off despite the seasonal fluctuation in furniture sales.

Despite the growing GMV, active buyers/sellers, and richer balance sheet, it was unsurprising that the stock continued to be discounted, attributed to the potential US tariff increases along with its operations in China, worsened by the still elevated short interest.

GCT 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, our concerns have materialized as GCT further pulled back by -40.4%, well underperforming the wider market at -11.2% and its well diversified commerce peers to a large degree.

It is apparent that the market has already turned pessimistic surrounding the furniture commerce company, as it fails to hold on to much of its 2024 stock price gains, worsened by the short seller attack in May 2024.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the potentially impacted consumer discretionary spending, thanks to the sticky inflationary pressures and the higher recessionary risks, worsened by the intensified tariff/trade war.

These developments have already triggered the possibility of borrowing costs being higher for longer, with the culmination of pessimistic developments contributing to the market-wide selloff since mid-February 2025, GCT included.

This is despite GCT's consistent outperformance over the past ten consecutive quarters (aside from the slight miss in FQ4 '24 bottom-lines).

This is on top of the robust FQ4 '24 revenues of $295.78M (-2.4% QoQ/+20.8% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 10.4% (-5.6 points QoQ/-7.5 YoY), and adj EPS of $0.75 (-34.7% QoQ/-29.9% YoY) merely suffering from the typical seasonality in large furniture purchases.

Otherwise, given the robust FY2024 numbers of +65.7% YoY, 13.5% (-3.3 points YoY), and +31.8% YoY, respectively, significantly aided by the full year of Noble House acquisition, we believe that GCT has outperformed indeed.

This is especially since GCT continues to expand its Marketplace Gross Merchandise Volume to $1.34B (+68.9% YoY) and 3P seller GigaCloud Marketplace GMV to $693.9M (+62.8% YoY).

This is on top of the growing active 3P sellers to 1,111 (+36.3% YoY) and higher active buyers at 9,306 (+85.7% YoY), well balancing the moderating spend per active buyer at $144.14K (-9% YoY).

Despite GCT's growing inventory levels of $172.48M (+30.4% YoY), readers must note that its inventory turnover ratio has improved to 3.42x in FY2024, compared to a year ago at 2.67x, underscoring its ability to "more efficiently sell the inventory it buys" while "not overspending on inventory purchases and is not incurring high storage and holding costs."

The management has also been diligently diversifying its global footprint, particularly in the EU, where the region reported +150% organic growth YoY in FY2024, building upon the region's "highest revenue growth" in FY2023.

On the other hand, readers may also want to note that the EU only comprises 16.3% of GCT's FY2024 revenues (+5.6 points YoY), with the bulk still attributed to the US at 75.9% (-2.8 points YoY).

With the US still being GCT's growth driver, as observed in the FY2024 revenue growth at +59% YoY/FY2023 at +39% YoY, it is undeniable that the ongoing uncertainties may trigger growth headwinds in FY2025.

This is especially since the new US administration "pushes aides to go bigger on tariffs as the key deadline nears," with the US consumer spending already recording the "first drop in almost two years."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This situation is especially worsened by the GCT's mixed FQ1 '25 revenue guidance of $257.5M at the midpoint (-12.9% QoQ/+5.2% YoY), with it naturally triggering questions surrounding its growth prospects after the robust 5Y historical top/bottom-line (adj EBITDA) expansion at a CAGR of +56.8%/+95%, respectively.

This is despite the management highlighting the minimal tariff impact on large and bulky furniture at supposedly "very, very low-single digit," with the sequential headwinds attributed to the macroeconomic uncertainties and the seasonality of its top-line driver segment, outdoor furniture sales.

Even so, given the tougher YoY comparisons after the accretive Noble House acquisition in November 2023, it appears that we are entering a painful growth normalization in 2025.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have lowered their forward estimates, with GCT expected to chart a decelerating top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +4.2%/+2.2% through FY2026, respectively, compared to the original estimates of +19.6%/+38.7%.

On the other hand, GCT has been able to report rich Free Cash Flow generation of $142.54M in FY2024 (+10.4% YoY), with the same expected over the next two years.

Combined with the inherent lack of debt and richer balance sheet at a cash position of $302.42M (+65% YoY), it is unsurprising that the management has been able to retire -1% of its float at an average price of $22.13 per share since the authorization of its new $46M share repurchase program on September 03, 2024.

With GCT trading way below those numbers, it appears that the management may possibly aggressively apply more of those repurchase authorizations at current levels, naturally returning great value to long-term shareholders.

So, Is GCT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GCT 2Y Stock Price

Even so, it is undeniable that GCT has had a painful -65.1% correction since the 2024 top, with it effectively losing -$1.2B of its market cap while consistently trading below its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

GCT Valuations

The same ongoing discount has been observed in GCT's FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 5.86x, down from the 1Y mean of 7.91x, the 2024 peaks of 18.76x, and the sector median of 16.37x.

GCT's oversold status has also been observed after comparing its FWD PEG GAAP ratio of 0.15x, against the sector median of 0.72x and its well diversified commerce peers, including Amazon (AMZN) at 0.39x, MercadoLibre (MELI) at 0.55x, and Sea Limited (SE) at 0.93x, with it underscoring the market's uncertainties surrounding the former's prospects.

If anything, despite the steep selloff thus far, its short interest ratio is still inflated at 20.7% as the CBOE Volatility Index remains elevated beyond the prior 125 trading days, with it signaling further volatility ahead.

Combined with the lack of floor from the meltdown and the ongoing macroeconomic/geopolitical headwinds, we believe that it may be more prudent to observe GCT from the sidelines for now, resulting in our reiterated Hold (Neutral) rating.

data.title: Dell Technologies Stock: This Capitulation May Be Your Chance
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

When you're an investment analyst, it's always frustrating to see one of your top picks declining further away from your price target - this is exactly what I feel looking at Dell Technologies (DELL) stock's price action since mid-2024:

Over the past 5 trading sessions, the DELL stock lost ~20% of its value amid the broader market's correction (the S&P 500 index (SPX) lost almost 8% for the same time), while on a YTD basis, the current correction of DELL looks like a real capitulation:

In times like today, real wealth is built - as investors say, one should buy when there's blood on the Street, and looking at Dell right now, we see a real bloodbath. However, I don't think that the firm has managed to lose its prospects; yes, there are concerns among analysts covering the tech sector regarding Microsoft's (MSFT) recent decisions to pull back from more data center leases, so it's also adding fuel to the fire. But I believe Dell investors shouldn't panic - the current margin of safety created after the latest dip creates a massive opportunity for dip buyers, in my opinion.

Why Do I Think So?

My main bullish thesis on Dell has been centered around its AI server business. Initially, for fiscal Q4 FY2025, the firm reported a $4.1 billion AI backlog, which then dramatically surged to ~$9 billion by late February 2025, fueled by major deals including one with xAI.

I also liked their financial developments in general with total revenue rising by 7% YoY in Q4 (to ~$23.9 billion) amid falling OPEX (-8% YoY), so DELL's resulting net income was on a steeper rise by the end of last fiscal year, showing a 27% YoY expansion. No growth deterioration in sight, as long as I can see it:

The EPS for Q4 managed to grow by 18% YoY and reached ~$2.68, beating the consensus estimate by $0.16 (or by 6.42%). It wasn't the largest beat on record, but compared to most tech firms out there, Dell's magnitude of earnings consensus beating seems remarkably strong given the firm's size.

I believe the AI momentum that we saw in 2H last calendar year is set to continue at least in the next few quarters. As Morgan Stanley's analyst Erik W. Woodring noted in late March (following meetings with Dell executives), the bank's internal checks pointed to a "healthy upside" versus Dell's already ambitious guidance of shipping at least $15 billion in AI servers in FY2026.

DELL's AI Factory, which extends beyond DELL's infrastructure and services offerings to include Nvidia (NVDA) components and software, is the framework DELL is leveraging to meet this demand and win AI deals with major T2 CSPs, including CoreWeave (CRWV) and xAI, as well as over 2,000 enterprise customers," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Erik Woodring, in a Thursday note.

As Dell continues to take advantage of the Tier 2 cloud service provider market, it should remain in a key position to enable AI for "years to come" Morgan Stanley noted, after hosting an investor meeting with Dell executives during Nvidia's GTC 2025.

Source: SA News

Investors seem to be worried about Dell's AI server gross margins potentially being in the mid-single digits, but I like Morgan Stanley's take on it. As Nvidia captures the majority of profits in the AI stack, they leave less for OEMs. But even at 1/3 the gross margin rate of traditional servers, Dell's AI server systems generate >100 times more profit dollars - the distinction between margin rate and absolute profit dollar contribution is fundamental to understanding Dell's strategy. So let me break this concept down with a simple example (the way I understood it). Imagine that a traditional server is sold for $15,000 at a GP margin of 30% - the GP profit equals $4,500. Now if Dell prices its AI server at $500,000 and gets a GP margin at 1/3 of what it could get from the traditional server (i.e. at 10%), they'll get $50,000 in absolute GP profits. The ">100x" figure that Morgan Stanley suggested paints an even more dramatic difference in reality. I know the price difference might seem unreal, but according to some third-party sources, my hypothetical calculations may be true.

High-end GPUs like NVIDIA A100 or H100 cost $10,000–$30,000 per unit. A single AI server rack with multiple GPUs can exceed $500,000

Source: Cyfuture

The TAM that Dell will have in the next few years from AI servers alone looks impressive: According to Research And Markets' data, the AI server market should be worth $837.83 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 34.3% between 2024 and 2030. What's not to like here?

As Morgan Stanley analysts proposed a potential path to over $10 in EPS for Dell in FY2026 "driven by AI momentum and sustained cost discipline" and BofA's analysts suggested that "the guide could be revised upwards through the year", the Street decided to punish the DELL stock and send its valuation to the extreme lows:

I thought that DELL was cheap in early March 2025 when it was trading slightly above 10x on the forwarding earnings. But now it's trading at ~7.6x after the dip occurred - and I see limited idiosyncratic fundamental changes to my reasoning.

Logically, I simply can't ignore the recently announced tariffs on goods imported to the US - but these changes are relevant to basically every US firm that manufactures/assembles its products before selling them in the US. Dell's revenue is ~50% dependent on US sales, but as they produce their products mostly in South Asia, they're going to pay "the new taxes" just like any other firm. But given Dell's recent moves to expand production in Mexico, probably they'll find a way to cope with the new reality (Mexico wasn't on Trump's list). Plus, I'd argue that the demand for highly priced offerings - the AI servers in particular - is less price elastic, meaning that Dell's partners are likely to take the final "price tag increase" as they have no choice (there will be no cheaper options on the market).

Given all that, I think that DELL now deserves a "Strong Buy" rating as never before.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The main risk factor right now is, of course, the net negative coming from Trump's tariffs implementation. It's unsurprising to see the markets' correction continuation in the past few trading sessions - the S&P 500 index and most other indices were at their ATHs and were destined to fall given the new circumstances. The inflation in the US will likely be higher, and that means higher inflation in most other regions of the world. And it also means that as the slowdown in the US (and also global) GDP growth - at least stagflation looks like a base-case scenario right now. If so, then DELL stock is likely to continue updating its local deeps - to reach the 2022 low of ~$32.9/share, it should correct by another ~54%. I do not see it even as my worst-case scenario for DELL, but who knows what might come next.

The Final Words

Despite the risk and depressed sentiment surrounding basically the whole tech sector as of today, I think that Dell Technologies is a great company, whose stock is now on a heavy dip and whose prospects are generally ignored or misunderstood by the market. The current FWD P/E ratio of 7.6x looks like a real bargain - even if Dell's FY2026 EPS misses the current consensus by 5%, the stock would look undervalued by over 25% assuming a P/E multiple of just 10x. So that's why I believe that the recent capitulation of the DELL stock price has brought a great buying opportunity for long-term investors.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: CVS Health's Trough Years Are Here - Double Digits Return Profile Ahead
data.description:
CVS Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Rally, With Double Digits Upside Ahead

We previously covered CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) in March 2024, discussing why its vertically integrated healthcare offerings continued to well balance each other as the increased healthcare utilizations triggered expanded pharmacy volumes, albeit an impacted bottom-line profile.

Given the stock's inherent undervaluation, secure dividend investment thesis, robust profitability, and healthy balance sheet, we reiterated our Buy rating then.

CVS 1Y Stock Price

Since then, CVS has breached its prior floor at $70s ranges, with the stock further pulling back to a 52-weeks low of $43.56 by mid-December 2024, near to Q2 '19 trading ranges, partly attributed to the Pharmacy Benefit Managers' headwinds and the legal headwinds surrounding unlawful opioid prescriptions.

With there appearing to be no further updates surrounding these issues (here and here), the stock has temporarily recovered to retest the next resistance levels of $68 ranges, significantly aided by the ongoing market rotation from tech/semiconductor stocks to value-/dividend-oriented stocks since mid-February 2025.

This is on top of CVS' double-beat FQ4'24 earnings performance, with revenues of $97.71B (+2.3% QoQ/+4.1% YoY), adj operating margins of 2.7% (+0.1 points QoQ/-1.7 YoY), and adj EPS of $1.19 (+9.1% QoQ/-43.8% YoY).

This is on top of the FY2024 numbers at $372.8B (+4.2% YoY), 3.2% (-1.6 points YoY/-2.7 from FY2019 levels of 5.9%), and adj EPS of $5.42 (-37.9% YoY), respectively, with the bottom-line headwinds naturally attributed to the higher medical costs as a percentage of premium revenues.

The same has been observed in CVS' drastically higher medical benefit ratio [MBR] at 94.8% in FQ4'24 (-0.4 points QoQ/+6.3 YoY) or 92.5% in FY2024 (+6.3 points YoY/ +8.3 from FY2019 levels of 84.2%), a trend similarly observed in its peers, albeit at a smaller extent, including:

  • UnitedHealth Group (UNH) at 85.5% in FY2024 and guidance of 86.5% in FY2025, compared to 82.5% in FY2019.
  • Centene (CNC) at 88.3% in FY2024, compared to 87.3% in FY2019.
  • Elevance Health (ELV) at 88.5% in FY2024, compared to 86.8% in FY2019.
  • Humana (HUM) at 89.7% in FY2024, compared to 85.6% in FY2019.

CVS' elevated MBR is partly attributed to the application of $670M in premium deficiency reserves recorded and the “unfavorable impact of the Company's Medicare Advantage star ratings for the 2024 payment year,” with the latter (redeterminations and higher medical costs) likely to be a new normal across the healthcare provider industry.

This is attributed to the reduced industrywide bonus payments of $11.8B in 2024 (-7.8% YoY), with a similar downtrend likely in 2025, pending any future changes in federal funding.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why CVS has offered a somewhat mixed FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $5.875 at the midpoint (+8.3% YoY) and cash flow from operations of $6.5B (-28.5% YoY), with the moderation in bottom-lines attributed to the normalization in healthcare costs, as discussed above.

These may have contributed to the downgraded consensus forward estimates, with the company expected to chart an impacted top bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5%/+13% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +6%/+21.1%, albeit improved from its 4Y historical growth (FY2019-FY2023) at +8.6%/+5.4% and the 10Y historical growth (FY2013-FY2023) at +10.9%/+8.1%, respectively.

Even so, CVS has already offered a promising FY2025 MBR guidance of 91.5% (-1 points YoY), with the higher healthcare costs likely to moderate ahead as they strive to deliver improved Healthcare Benefits margins over the next few years.

This is up from the 0.23% reported in FY2024 (-4.97 points YoY/-7.17 from FY2019 levels of 7.4%) and nearer to its long-term target margins of 3% to 5%, with FY2024/FY2025 likely to be trough years before the emergence of improved YoY comparison ahead.

Much of our optimism arises from CVS' growing medical memberships by +5.4% YoY along with the price updates by “mid-4% rate increases” in the Medicaid and (increase unspecified) in the individual insurance businesses, allowing them to deliver improved bottom-lines in FY2025.

At the same time, we believe that the company remains well positioned to weather the ongoing healthcare industry transition and insurance headwinds, as observed in the somewhat stable net debt levels of -$51.65B (-8.9% YoY/+7.5% from FY2019 levels of -$55.86B).

The same has been observed in the FY2024 Free Cash Flow generation of $6.32B (-39.1% YoY) and only $3.62B of its debts maturing within the year, allowing them to service the annualized dividend obligations of $3.37B (+7.6% YoY).

CVS Valuations

These promising reasons may also be why CVS has recovered as it has, with it triggering the upgrade in its FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 11.52x, up from the 1Y mean of 8.90x and 5Y mean of 9.85x, while nearing the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.37x.

Despite this, CVS continues to appear attractive at a FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.50x when compared to its 5Y mean of 1.78x and 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 1.40x.

The same conclusion may also be derived when comparing to the sector median PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.66x and its healthcare provider peers, including UNH at 1.59x, CNC at 0.72x, ELV at 1.24x, and HUM at 1.68x, with it offering interested investors an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is CVS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CVS 5Y Stock Price

For now, CVS has had an excellent recovery by +56.2% since the worst of the December 2024 selloff, with the stock now trading well above its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $5.875 at the midpoint (+8.3% YoY) and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.52x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to our fair value estimates of $67.60.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $7.82, there remains an excellent upside potential of +32.2% to our base-case long-term price target of $90.

Assuming a further upward rerating in CVS' valuations nearer to its 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 13.37x, there may be an even richer upside potential of +53.5% to our bull-case long-term price target of $104.50 as well.

While the recent stock price recovery has triggered the moderation in its forward dividend yields to 3.91%, down from the recent heights of 6.08%, the stock continues to offer a relatively decent income investment thesis compared to the 5Y averages of 2.58% and the sector median of 1.62%.

These developments underscore why we are rating the CVS stock as a Buy here, thanks to the rich dual-pronged return prospects from capital appreciation and dividend incomes.

data.title: Bad News For The Market, Good News For Retirement Income
data.description:
Who could have thought that an easier policy decision-making environment for the new Administration would lead to such a shock therapy both in the economy and in the market?

We have gone from an initial optimism and further increasing stock prices to very steep daily declines coupled with a relatively consistent uncertainty pertaining to the new tariffs, response from other trade partners and the corresponding effects to the U.S. businesses.

The practical implications from these dynamics are quite many, but these would be the most important ones (at least in the context of capital allocation/investing):

  • Depressed capital markets and M&A activity, which, among other things, limits the access to equity capital for funding growth.
  • Cost uncertainty, which makes it extremely difficult to make final investment decisions (this leads to negative knock-on effects across the board).
  • Export market uncertainty as there is an increased chatter around the world (or tariff-affected partners) on what kind of measures to implement in order to respond with reciprocal harm.

It finally feels like we are about to deal with something meaningful, where the "Fed put" will not be able to come to a rescue. The Fed has really no options to stimulate the market without increasing the risk for a rising inflation, which is the last thing what we want now.

In a nutshell, we are experiencing a shock therapy that is driven by real risks and real headwinds to the underlying business fundamentals as opposed to some changes in the yield-curve or market's expectations around future inflation levels.

What it means for investors?

When it comes to growth: nothing good. It is too early to tell, but many signs pinpoint to a period of more pragmatic valuations and "down-to-earth" growth expectations.

When it comes to value: interesting opportunities. Here we have to delineate between high-quality and speculative value.

The former is where I would feel comfortable deploying capital, while the latter is a clear no-go. When we have such a volatile economy and forthcoming pressures on business cash flows, investing in companies with no margin of safety would be like driving up the hill without having breaks.

Conversely, being invested in high-quality value can materially increase the odds for outperforming the rest of the market or at least avoiding notable price declines in the portfolio. What it can also provide is a more enticing initial yield level (as a result of the market correction) that is very important for income-oriented investors.

So, high-quality value, in my view, offers two strategic advantages:

  1. Capital preservation.
  2. Attractive current income streams to collect, while weathering the storm in the economy/market.

Both of these points directly support durable income investing strategies. For example, retirement investors, who seek reasonable cash flows from their portfolios, while keeping distribution cut risks as far away as possible, are now very well-positioned (i.e., no major changes are required, only a firm commitment to the already chosen strategy).

However, as outlined earlier, it is very important to understand the underlying quality of these value/income-tilted investors as having a decent margin of safety in place has, arguably, become just as important as in the COVID-19 period.

Let me now explain what I mean by "margin of safety" and provide a couple of security picks that check those boxes.

Investing with a margin of safety

All in all, to capture truly sustainable and non-cyclical dividends, we have to go back to the basics. It is about the business model, cash flows and leverage.

When the economy goes south and the demand weakens across the board most businesses suffer. Yet, the ones that suffer the most are those that operate in highly cyclical industries, offering products and services that can be easily substituted with cheaper ones or completely avoided by more cautious consumers.

Conversely, businesses that operate in the system-critical and durable demand industries can nicely benefit from not having to worry about losing great chunks of their sales.

My favorite segments or asset classes in this category are REITs and infrastructure (MLPs included).

The thing with most REITs is that they have secured their future cash flows from a diversified mix of tenants that have stipulated long-term rent agreements with the property owners (i.e., REITs). Granted, it depends on what kind of REIT sectors we are talking about, as, for example, low-quality office, medical and high-end residential areas might still be left unprotected.

Agree Realty (ADC) is a great example, which has ~10 years of a weighted average lease term with a diversified pool of durable retail tenants, an almost fully occupied portfolio and the lion's share of its tenants carrying investment grade balance sheets.

Infrastructure is another defensive area, which is largely unaffected by the changes in the consumer behavior. Irrespective of what is happening with the tariffs, import of goods or costs, there will always be a demand for roads, utilities, midstream, telecommunication towers and data centers. It might be fair to lower the near- to medium-term growth trajectory for infrastructure businesses as new projects could get delayed, but what has already been built or in a construction (with positive FID) should remain in the "business", generating stable cash flows. It is a similar case to REITs, where the cash is coming in from long-term agreements with multiple tenants (in this case bankable offtakers).

Currently, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) is my favorite pick that carries a diversified critical infrastructure portfolio. A large part of the current cash flows and the majority of the CapEx pipeline is associated with power utility and data consumption infrastructure that is already contracted with a list of blue-chip businesses. Plus, almost all of the contracts come with embedded CPI escalators, which in combination with about one third of a retained FFO generation would allow BIP to register at least inflation-level growth in case the new project pipeline gets paused for some period of time.

Besides having the right exposure sector-wise, it is just as important (if even not more important) to be invested in businesses that embody resilient fundamentals. To put it simply, in the times of elevated probabilities for a distress, a strong cushion on the cash flows and capital structure can be a game changer in the context of dividend sustainability and the ability to drive incremental growth.

The following aspects are what I would encourage prudent income investors to consider (or put an emphasis on) when allocating capital these days:

  • Investment grade balance sheet (an upper IG level would be an even better advantage).
  • Well-laddered debt maturity profile.
  • Conservative cash flow payout ratio (e.g., at least one third of the cash generation retained undistributed).
  • Strong customer base (e.g., diversification, long-term contracts, regulated cash flows or investment grade tenants).

Two businesses that fulfill these criteria and that could also be placed in a "durable business model" category are Realty Income Corporation (O) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) (REIT and MLP, respectively). They both have upper investment grade balance sheets, well-laddered borrowing structures, ample margin of safety on the distribution coverage front and defensive cash flows. They have made consistent distributions for more than 20 years in a row and I see no reason for an unexpected cut or a sudden slow-down in the dividend growth process.

The bottom-line

Finally, we see a shift from growth to value.

What the current policy has done is it has reminded investors about the importance of a margin of safety. Similarly, it has also provided a ground for the market to become more pragmatic in the way it assigns valuation multiples for growth-tilted securities.

In my view, this is an excellent moment, when to remain committed to a durable income strategy. High-quality value and sustainable income strategies are well-positioned to shield invested capital and keep the current income flows in place.

Apart from the commitment to such a strategy, it is equally important to make sure that the portfolio investments are properly calibrated. With this, I mean being invested in non-cyclical industries through fundamentally resilient businesses that have robust capital structures and strong cash flows.

data.title: Penguin Solutions: Growth Momentum Still On Track
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Penguin Solutions (PENG) on 10th January, with my key thesis being that the business has a very strong growth potential, stemming from the ongoing momentum in AI-related infrastructure investments. After looking at the 2Q25 results, I believe that my thesis is still valid. PENG showed solid revenue growth acceleration, and management’s pipeline commentary reinforces that there is still ongoing momentum.

Earnings results update

In the latest quarter (2Q25) reported two days ago, PENG reported $366 million in total revenue (up 28% y/y), with Advanced Computing [AS] contributing $200 million, up 42% y/y; Integrated Memory [IM] contributing $105 million, up 26% y/y; and Optimized Led flat y/y at $60 million. Gross margins have also improved. Adj. gross margin came in at 30.8%, up from 30.1% in 1Q25 and 30% in 4Q24. Both top-line growth and gross margin expansion resulted in adj EBIT almost doubling from $27 million in 2Q24 to $49 million in 2Q25. Adj EPS also nearly doubled y/y to $0.52. Looking ahead, management has raised their FY25 revenue growth outlook from 15% to 17% while reaffirming its full-year EPS guidance of $1.60 +/- $0.10 and gross margin expectations of 31% +/- 1%.

Hyperscaler demand is intact

Over the past two months, there has been a lot of chatter (like this and this) about demand for AI-related investment. Demand is actually not as great as one would have thought, and this has impacted the share price sentiment of PENG, especially given its exposure to hyperscaler demand. I believe PENG’s 2Q25 results should have put an end to the narrative that demand is weak. In the call, management confirmed strong demand from a Tier 1 hyperscaler and noted “exciting wins at existing customers.” It also added three new logos across the technology, media, and telecom (TMT) and federal sectors. Management’s tone and comments tell me that demand has not seen any form of weakening at all.

In fact, I am convinced that demand for PENG’s solution has gotten stronger from this comment: “As customers move from proof of concept to full-scale AI implementations, we're seeing signs that we have entered the early stages of growth and corporate buildups at scale and are excited for what lies ahead.” This is a big deal because it suggests that PENG is going to see a strong ramp-up in revenue, likely at great magnitudes vs. what it is achieving today, given management used the term “full-scale AI implementations.” In my view, this transition is the strongest validation of PENG’s product-market fit to date.

Another encouraging sign is that PENG is finding success in developing solutions that, I believe, will help it win more deals. The first highlight is that in the quarter, PENG expanded its ICE ClusterWare AIM Service (this is basically PENG’s in-house software that enables predictive automation across many hardware platforms like NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel). This deserves highlighting, because managing heterogeneous compute environments is increasingly challenging for large-scale AI deployments. Hence, my opinion is that if PENG’s solution can support multiple architectures, it should have a technical lead vs. solutions that can’t, and that is a comparative advantage that PENG can lean on to win more deals.

The pipeline suggests this momentum can continue

Finally, and perhaps the most important takeaway was the revenue growth visibility that management provided for 2H25 and beyond. They noted, “We have seen growth in our pipeline during the first half of fiscal year 2025 and remain focused on working to convert these opportunities into bookings over the remainder of the fiscal year and into fiscal year 2026.” This may have sounded like a typical pipeline commentary, but I think it says a lot more when we consider that y/y revenue growth accelerated by 400 bps sequentially (2Q25 28% y/y growth is the highest since 2Q22). To me, this sounds more like PENG is seeing better underlying demand momentum. The fact that management explicitly mentions converting pipeline into FY26 bookings also gives me confidence that PENG has multi-quarter visibility, which instills confidence (especially when there is a lot of noise regarding AI capex demand).

If PENG can continue converting pipeline to revenue at the same pace we saw this quarter, I think there’s a strong case for sustained growth into FY26. And with more visibility now layered into PENG’s equity story, I wouldn’t be surprised if sentiment starts to shift more positively from here.

Valuation

I believe the recent market turmoil caused by the Trump administration's sweeping tariffs executive order has created an attractive entry point to invest in PENG. The stock’s valuation has been beaten down from ~1x forward revenue to 0.7x, which doesn’t make sense given the improving fundamentals and better visibility into forward growth. I don’t see the tariffs structurally impacting the demand for AI-related CAPEX either. In my opinion, this sell-down is due to investors becoming risk-averse (i.e., the market sell-down caused this and is not isolated to PENG). If we look over the next few years, I think there is a very strong likelihood that PENG will report accelerating revenue growth. I modeled 20% growth in FY26/27, but it could be too conservative considering 1H25 grew 26% y/y. Like I have said before, multiples should re-rate higher to 1.2x PENG to convince the market that it can sustain growth at high-20%s.

Investment Risk

It is hard to track actual AI-related CAPEX spending, and it is proven that certain news flow can hurt overall sentiment, which is bad for PENG. PENG’s share price could see periods of violent sell-off in between quarterly results. Even if demand is still intact, there could be periods of lumpiness, and if PENG reports figures that are below expectations (not because demand has softened, but because of timing), the market could take it as a negative signal.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on PENG because this quarter confirmed that PENG is still seeing strong AI-related demand. The financials are moving in the right direction, in that growth has accelerated as I expected. More importantly, the tone from management instills confidence that AI pipeline conversions will continue into FY26. In my view, this update should have removed any doubt about whether demand is softening.

data.title: Toromont: Digging Those Dividend Increases
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Buoyed by strong demand for heavy equipment to support public infrastructure development and mining operations in Eastern Canada, Toromont Industries Ltd. (TSX:TIH:CA) has leveraged its position as the exclusive dealer of Caterpillar to steadily improve its earnings profile. Despite the current uncertainty overhanging from the threat of tariffs, Toromont has a strong order backlog and a support-oriented business model that generates substantial recurring revenue. The strong free cash flow generated from this business model has supported 56 years of consistent dividend payments. Extending a 36-year record of annual dividend increases, this dividend aristocrat recently rewarded investors with an 8.3% increase to its quarterly payout.

Company Profile

With a market capitalization of approximately $9B, Toromont is one of the largest Caterpillar heavy equipment dealers in North America. Based in Ontario, the company operates a network of branches across Ontario, Manitoba, Newfoundland, and Nunavut. Through the 2017 acquisition of Hewitt for approximately $1B, Toromont also operates in Québec and the Atlantic Canada.

With business segments offering new and used equipment sales as well as a large rental fleet, Toromont serves a broad market. Leveraging digital technologies to enhance fleet monitoring and equipment performance and positioning, Toromont continues to enhance the prominence of its service and product support division. Product support accounted for 40% of revenue in 2024. This compares to 34% of revenue derived from support in the years 2014-2016.

Notably, CIMCO, the company's industrial refrigeration business, has grown from 4% of revenue in FY 2022 to 9% for FY 2024, including packages and support. CIMCO designs and installs remigration systems for commercial clients, including food processing and ice rinks. With strong growth from its service segment in 2024, CIMCO improved revenue by 16% over the previous year. Healthy recreational demand has generated a backlog of $342MM for the division, up 34% over the previous year.

Outlook for 2025

In 2024, Toromont generated $5B in revenue, a 9% increase over the previous year. At the same time, net earnings declined 4.3% year over year on higher costs. Following this year of healthy sales, the company may be in for a more volatile 2025. Global trade tensions may defer capital spending and project investment in the short term. Given this uncertainty, Toromont may look to focus on its rental and used equipment sales channels.

Fortunately, Toromont is supported in the short term by a significant order backlog. At the end of Q4 2024, Toromont enjoyed an order backlog of $1.1B. With inventory levels improved by 32% over the precious year, the company anticipates that 90% is expected to be delivered in the next 12 months. A growing emphasis on parts and service over sales has helped to diversify revenue in recent years. Critically, the company has developed a strong source of recurring revenue. This is a helpful foundation from which to navigate the uncertainty of the Canada-U.S. trade war and a weak Canadian dollar.

Returning Capital to Shareholders

Toromont has an impressive dividend profile that combines a decades-long record of double-digit dividend growth, 56 years of consistent payments, and a low payout ratio.

Marking 36 years of consecutive annual dividend increases, in February 2025, Toromont raised its quarterly dividend by $0.04 to $0.52 per share in 2024. This latest increase of 8.3% extends an impressive dividend growth pattern characterized by the company's 5-year dividend CAGR of nearly 12%. While the current yield of 1.85% is modest, the low payout ratio of just 22% indicates the long-term dividend growth potential has plenty of runways.

In addition to its dividend commitment, Toromont returns capital to shareholders through its share buyback program. In 2024, Toromont repurchased 1,321,500 shares for $160.4 million under its NCIB. The current NCIB, which expires in September 2025, permits the company to buyback up to 8,180,146 common shares, representing 10% of Toromont's public float.

Valuation

Toromont trades at 17X forward earnings, an 11% discount to the company's 5-year average P/E ratio of almost 20X. This compares to Finning International Inc. (FTT:CA), Toromont's rival in Western Canada, which trades at just 10X 2025 consensus earnings. This valuation premium over Finning is warranted by the company's broader sector mix. Finning's concentration on western Canada and South America means that approximately half of revenue is derived from the mining industry, compared to just 22% from Toromont.

Related to the two companies' revenue mixes, Toromont has enjoyed steadier growth, with 5-year revenue growth at over 9%. Finning on the other hand, has been more volatile, with 5-year average growth of approximately 18% dropping to just 6% in the last 12 months. Toromont's business mix has enabled consistently strong EBITDA margins over the last 5 years, approximately 14%, well ahead of Finning's 8%. While Finning is the larger of the two dealers by revenue and enterprise value, Finning's reliance on debt in its capital structure means that Toromont has a larger market capitalization.

Risk Analysis

Toromont is a debt-averse company, with an enviable balance sheet. With approximately $900MM in cash and cash equivalents, Toromont has a negative net debt position of $200MM. The company has access to another $250MM in available credit through an accordion arrangement and $460MM in a revolving credit facility. As a result of its strong balance sheet and ample liquidity, Morningstar DBRS has assigned Toromont a credit rating of A [Low] with stable trends.

Heavy equipment is a cyclical business that can be impacted by localized and macroeconomic forces. Toromont's geographic diversification serves to alleviate dependence on any single localized industry. The company's growing parts and service segment acts as a driver of recurring revenue and serves to lessen reliance on equipment sales for revenue growth.

With most CAT products sold in Canada, manufactured in the U.S., there is a risk that heavy machinery originating in the U.S. could be subject to Canadian counter tariffs. Despite efforts to mitigate the impact of tariffs by stockpiling inventory, long lead times on equipment and to a lesser extent, on parts, means that Toromont has limited ability to preempt tariffs by rushing orders. With inventories turning over 3–4 times per year, the impact of these efforts is short-term. Along with monitoring tariffs, Toromont has been employing hedging practices to manage bottom-line exposure to fluctuating exchange rates.

Caterpillar Brand

As the exclusive caterpillar dealer for Eastern Canada, Toromont is closely aligned with its single product line and is subject to acute brand, reputation and supply risks through this concentration into a single supplier. Cat is a market leader in the heavy equipment sector, with market share approaching 20% across many of its product lines.

Caterpillar's brand is broadly recognized and associated with quality and durability. According to Caterpillar, the brand is valued at $8.1B USD, earning it a spot in the Top 100 Best Global Brand list for the 22nd year in a row. This brand value has been extended to other licensing and merchandising opportunities. The company boasts 86,000 retail outlets globally selling Cat-licensed merchandise, including 215 stand-alone Cat Merchandise Stores. Together, these retailers sold $2.95B USD of Cat licensed merchandise in 2023.

Investor Takeaways

Toromont enjoys the privileged position of being the exclusive distributor of CAT products in Eastern Canada. This attractive market exclusivity and geographic diversification make Toromont's business perennially attractive. The company's strong order backlog and high portion of recurring revenue through its product support division help to mitigate the uncertainty associated with the impact of a trade war.

With low debt levels and a commitment to growing its dividend, Toromont remains attractive for long-term investors. The current market uncertainty may present a more attractive entry point and the opportunity to pick up shares at a higher than average dividend yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nest Egg Portfolio Update: Remain Steadfast
data.description:
Thesis

Back in January, I wrote an article discussing my approach to building a diversified nest egg portfolio by investing in four ETFs. Since this article was posted, I have contributed $300 dollars each month, following the portfolio allocation breakdown and challenging myself to invest automatically each month regardless of market conditions. Thus far, my portfolio has generated a negative return, with Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD) performing the best out of the selected ETFs. This is mainly due to the total market turmoil that we have experienced in recent months. After outlining this portfolio challenge, I received numerous comments outlining the risk of being invested collectively in Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF Shares (VTI), and Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund ETF Shares (VGT) due to the high concentration of technology, overlap among the top holdings, and heavier weighting towards a handful of companies. However, given the current market turmoil, I believe the best approach right now is to remain steadfast in accumulation and continue to buy shares of the market over the long term.

Previous Article

In my last article, Implementing A Four-ETF Portfolio Strategy To Build A Nest Egg, I outlined the four-ETF approach to building a nest egg portfolio, which included investing into a portfolio that holds VOO, VTI, VGT, and SCHD with the following allocation breakdown: 30%, 30%, 15%, and 25%, respectively. My overall notion was that if I kept investing in this ETF portfolio each month, I would eventually have a nest egg to fall back on later in life from an automatic and passive approach to investing. Moreover, by investing in a group of ETFs rather than just a single market index, I anticipated this portfolio to have more diversification in times of market distress in order to help weather the volatility and to have the chance of generating slightly higher returns than the S&P 500 given a small allocation to higher risk. Now, since this first article, the market has been a little choppy. We have seen consistent selloffs, good companies getting crushed after reporting good earnings, a Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK) flash scare with shares of NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) which is one of the top holdings of the ETFs in this portfolio, and a change in the U.S. tariffs causing another meltdown. Even with all this market turmoil, I’m continuing the path to a nest egg portfolio by accumulating shares of these four ETFs, and I’m confident that I’m buying shares at a lower price today than what we will see in the future.

Current Portfolio

Thus far, I have invested $1200 since the start of this challenge and have received a total of $4.33 in dividends that were reinvested, which brings my total capital invested to $1204.33. My total portfolio value before the market opened on April 3rd sits at $1174.87 with a negative 2.45% [($1174.87-$1204.33)/$1204.33] total return and current holdings of 0.677 shares of VOO, 1.263 shares of VTI, 10.88 shares of SCHD, and 0.307 shares of VGT. Although this is not an explicit goal of the portfolio, I’m also collecting dividends from each one of these holdings. Currently, we will receive roughly $20 in dividends over the next year from this allocation without contributing any more capital. For this challenge, I have dividend reinvestments turned on, and overtime the dividends will also contribute to the total number of shares accumulated. This is commonly referred to as the DRIP method and is one way in which we can receive an even higher level of a compound return on our capital invested today. One consideration with dividends, however, is that there are tax implications if this portfolio is being constructed in a normal brokerage account rather than a Roth account. After the market turmoil this week, I anticipate being down further but will look forward to buying shares at lower prices. As you can see in the chart below, the market, denoted by the S&P 500, is down year to date, as are most of the portfolio holdings, except for SCHD, which is up 2%.

Current Considerations

With the imposed tariffs on April 2nd and the market sell off shortly after, this has opened the door for buying shares of securities at lower prices. However, even with the selloff we’ve already seen this year, we are still in a very bullish environment, and stocks continue to be disconnected from fundamental valuations. When evaluating the price/earnings ((P/E)) ratios of the ETFs in this portfolio, they are still trading near 10-year highs. For example, the current P/E of the S&P 500 ETF VOO is 23.93, the current P/E of the risky allocation ETF VGT is 33.3, and the current P/E of the more diversified market ETF VTI is 24.44. However, SCHD which I used to diversify into long-term dividend-paying value companies, is currently trading closer to an average P/E of 15.5. Given these ratios, I’m not concerned about what seems to be continued overvaluation in the market, as I have a long-term time horizon and I don’t believe I will be able to time a market top or bottom. Given the already over 10% discount due to the sell-off, I’m glad to buy at these lower prices. However, with how shaky the market has been and the impact of the current economic environment on company earnings, further decline from here is possible.

Another fundamental that I’ve taken into consideration when evaluating ETFs is the current dividend yield compared to their historical dividend yield. This is a measure by which I’m attempting to evaluate how expensive the current price of the ETFs is compared to their historical value. Currently, VOO, VGT, and VTI are trading close to historically lower levels of dividend yields, while SCHD holds a better yield on current price. As we have come to understand, with the higher P/E multiples of the ETFs, there is room for further reversion back to the historical mean.

Now, given what I’ve highlighted above, you may be asking, “should I change my current allocation strategy?” I don’t believe so. As mentioned in my previous article, Warren Buffett, who some consider to be the world’s best investor, has noted before that long-term investment into index funds is the best thing to do for most investors.

By following this allocation strategy of the four ETFs approach, I'm generally following this advice and continuing to buy the best companies over time with the same amount of capital each month. As prices fluctuate, I should accumulate shares at an average price over the long term to produce what would be considered market-level returns at a low cost as compared to actively managed funds. However, one decision an investor could make when the market selloffs continue to worsen is whether you’re willing to buy an additional share, or contribute an additional monthly allocation, to further increase your long-term wealth accumulation. Personally, I will look to add to the current portfolio strategy as the market continues to recede from historically high multiples, even though there may be more downsides ahead.

Only Invest What You’re Willing To Lose

This is a little counterintuitive, but I follow a saying of only investing what I’m willing to lose. With a long-term time horizon, steady income unrelated to the market from full-time employment, and an appropriate savings rate, my $300 dollar allocation is what I’m willing to risk in order to generate long-term wealth. By forgoing consumption today and living below my means, I’m able to allocate a portion of my income towards my goal of wealth creation, but if we had a market crash, I would not panic sell the shares I've accumulated because I don’t need the money in the next few years. I believe that I’m doing the appropriate research and avoiding common pitfalls of some individual investors through diversification to avoid the possibility of total investment loss on a single company, but sometimes the market takes on a mind of its own. One thing investors need to understand is their own risk appetite when making any investment allocation or decision. If an intraday 2-3% decline causes you to stare at the fluctuating prices, you may need to dial down the risk. For me, I’m able to allocate $300 at a set it and forget it mentality, and if the market continues to decline this year, I know I will buy more shares at lower prices.

Conclusion

Although this ETF portfolio has produced negative overall returns in the short term, I believe that I will follow the market returns, or slightly outpace the market, over the long term. The goal of this ETF is to build long-term wealth at a set-it-and-forget it method by investing in the market with the same allocation each month. As you can see in the first chart above, if I were only invested in one of these ETFs, the total returns this year would be significantly different, and some downside has been avoided by holding SCHD as part of this attempt at diversification. In times of turmoil, like we've experienced this year, it may be good to consider adding an additional allocation during large down days to enhance overall accumulation over time, as it’s very difficult to perfectly time the market. Before making investment and allocation decisions, investors need to understand their own risk tolerance and do their own research. This article is solely based on my research, incorporating my own risk tolerance, and based on my current understanding of market conditions.

data.title: Prologis: Time To Hold, Not To Panic
data.description:
Since March, Prologis's (PLD) stock has been down roughly -19%. Industrial and retail REITs are potentially the core REIT categories most affected by tariffs due to their dependence on global trade and consumer spending. Just the day after the reciprocal tariffs announcement, the stock price fell -9.57%, and in this analysis, I will explain why I consider the stock a "hold."

Company Description

Prologis is the largest industrial REIT in the world, and with over $150 billion, ranks second in enterprise value after American Tower (AMT). They operate 5,866 buildings, which translates into $6.2 billion of net operating income, and have $136 billion in assets under management in their Strategic Capital segment, where they act as an asset manager through private funds and REITs.

They have a diversified tenant base across different industries, and their top ten customers combined do not represent more than 15% of the net effective base. At the same time, they have global operations in Europe, Asia, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil, but all of these combined represented just 14% of the net operating income in 2024.

Effects on the Economy

The topic of discussion that has been moving markets recently has been the effect that tariffs could bring on the economy. Two main macro factors move industrial REIT demand: GDP growth and consumer spending. Although there isn't hard data confirming that these are in trouble, as tariffs just came into effect recently, soft data such as GDPNow and analysts' forecasts have a negative sentiment on the level of GDP growth.

In late February, GDPNow started forecasting a real GDP contraction of -1.5% in Q1 2025, which has expanded to the most recent figure of -3.7%. At the same time, analysts from Goldman Sachs (GS) have trimmed their GDP growth expectations for FY 2025 from 2.4% to 1.7% due to the effect that tariffs could bring.

In addition, the Michigan Consumer Sentiment for March contracted to 52.6, which is the lowest level over the past two years, suggesting that consumers believe that the incremental cost of tariffs won't be offset by faster wage growth. At the same time, retail sales in February saw a slight rise of 0.2%, which was significantly below the 0.6% forecast.

Theoretical tariffs encourage US companies to move more of their production to the United States, which could boost industrial real estate demand, assuming a constant exchange rate. Nonetheless, tariffs will likely lower the short-run aggregate supply, impacting industrial renewal rates and pressuring mark-to-market rates downwards. Although Prologis is globally diversified, just 8.4% of the FY 2024 NOI came from operations outside the United States, signaling a higher tariff impact on tenants across most of its operations.

Attractive Core FFO Growth

Despite being a cyclical business, Prologis is a REIT with constant developments, which has allowed the funds from operations to grow sequentially every year. From 2001 to 2024, Prologis invested more than $46.8 billion in developments, with 68% of them coming from outside the United States, and generated a value creation of $13.6 billion. These developments and rent increases have allowed PLD to achieve an attractive core FFO 5-year CAGR of 10.7%.

At the same time, Prologis has a sound balance sheet to face a potential recession, with debt constituting 26% of the year-end market cap, a conservative debt-to-adjusted EBITDA of just 4.6x, and a weighted average debt maturity of 9 years.

Shares Remain Discounted

In the most recent fiscal year, Prologis achieved core funds from operations of $5.71 per share, which implies a price-to-core FFO multiple of 18.2x, which is -32% lower than its 5-year historical average of 26.7x, but higher than peers such as Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) at 15.7x and STAG Industrial (STAG) at 14.5x.

Simultaneously, Prologis' dividend yield of 3.83% is almost 100 bps above its historical average at a payout ratio of 70.8% but remains below its peers' average dividend yield of 4.5%. Therefore, Prologis is undervalued compared to its history and remains slightly more expensive than its peers, but not by a significant difference, even after considering the size premium embedded in Prologis' valuation.

Risks

Despite the increase in credit spreads, the decrease in the 10-year yield has been larger year-to-date, making the US Corporate A Effective Yield decrease, allowing the cost of borrowing Prologis to be cheaper. Nonetheless, these yields remain well above the average of the past decade. At the same time, with tariffs, development would likely continue to be slow as raw materials for construction get more expensive, and trade disruptions could decrease demand for warehouses, pressuring mark-to-market rents to the downside.

Takeaway & Stock Rating

Certainly, Prologis has a strong balance sheet and is a well-positioned REIT to face potential recessionary risks. As an industrial REIT, they are likely to be affected by slowdowns in global trade, and with the cost of borrowing remaining high, the outlook is not the most optimistic in the near term. Nonetheless, with a valuation that is -32% discount to its history and a strong track record with an attractive core-FFO per share growth, I will rate the shares of Prologis as a "hold."

data.title: Trump's Tariffs: The Impact On Fixed Income
data.description:
By Ashok Bhatia, CFA

New tariffs could hamper U.S. economic growth and change market dynamics for the long term.

On Wednesday, U.S. President Donald J. Trump introduced his much-anticipated tariff regime. All imports would be subject to a 10% tariff, while Trump would impose higher rates on nations that the administration views as “bad actors” on trade.

We wanted to share our perspective on the tariff news and the implications for fixed income investments.

Macro and Rates

  • If the tariffs are implemented next week as announced, we would expect a U.S. GDP growth rate of below 1% and core inflation of around 3.5% this year. This would represent a substantial deterioration in both GDP and inflation outcomes relative to general expectations. In our view, a recession (if defined as negative growth rates) would be a real possibility.
  • We have expected the Federal Reserve to ease two to three times this year as growth (without any tariff news) is already slowing, something that should continue. In our view, the Fed will be balancing the tariffs’ impact on inflation and inflation expectations with any growth deterioration. Inflation expectations appear well anchored, which creates scope for the Fed to respond to the growth changes if needed. We believe that the market is priced for 100 basis points of easing over the next 12 months.
  • We expect a similar response from other central banks like the European Central Bank and Bank of England that will balance the inflation impacts with the growth impacts.

Credit Implications

  • Regarding credit markets, we generally have maintained reduced exposures and an up-in-quality bias so far this year. Despite the widening of credit spreads year-to-date, we are generally maintaining this strategic positioning. We continue to think credit spreads should have a wider equilibrium level to better price in the probability of a recession. In our view, for example, U.S. high yield spreads of around 425 basis points would better reflect this risk than the current 370 basis points.
  • Yesterday, we saw pricing pressure in credit markets, but it was generally modest. Businesses with supply chains in Asia were the most affected, but overall, we would characterize the widening in spreads as orderly. That said, there was some evidence of forced liquidation of positions in derivatives markets.
  • In emerging markets, we generally have been underweight Asia and overweight Latin America, and are maintaining this exposure. We are seeing opportunities to slightly reduce emerging markets corporate risk given the strong performance this year.

Looking Ahead

In the near term, we think it is more likely than not that we see negotiation and some watering down of the announced tariffs, but the directional impulse of these policies is unlikely to change. Interestingly, the relatively favorable treatment of Canada, Mexico and Latin America suggests a deliberate policy of favoring the U.S. “sphere of influence” in a multipolar world.

Longer term, we believe that a world with more U.S. trade barriers will also be a world where the U.S. likely sees fewer capital inflows and a higher equilibrium cost of capital. Implications of this could be higher U.S. rates than perhaps expected and steeper yield curves and a weaker dollar, as well as an increased risk premium in credit markets. Tax cuts, deregulation and pro-growth policies may partially offset this impulse, but global capital flows will likely change over the coming years and could result in new equilibrium levels.

Similarly, markets outside the U.S. will likely be viewed more favorably. Outperformance of European credit markets and emerging markets, compared to the U.S. markets, could continue.
